verified_guards: runtime/zig/tools/check_core_import_fence.py currently enforces core/full separation, synthetic runtime-state bans, compatibility-facade bans, and non-backend reach-through.
python3 runtime/zig/tools/check_core_import_fence.py exits 0 in the current tree.
note: Temporary runtime/zig architecture and adoption audit. Findings may capture confirmed defects or strategic Zig-adoption backlog items, and are ordered by priority_rank.

summary: The exported Metal pipeline-cache constructor no longer stores allocator state backed by a stack-local GPA.
why: `MetalPipelineCache` stores the allocator and later uses it in `deinit`, `register_compute_key`, warmup-manifest IO, and path allocation. Returning after constructing the cache with a stack-local GPA made allocator state invalid and could corrupt later allocations or frees.
exactly_what_to_do: Delete the stack-local GPA from `doeNativeMetalPipelineCacheCreate`.
Plumb an explicit long-lived allocator from the owning runtime into the pipeline cache, or introduce a dedicated process-lifetime cache allocator module with explicit init/deinit semantics.
Add a regression test that creates, uses, releases, recreates, and re-releases the cache while touching allocator-backed fields.
acceptance_criteria: No exported constructor stores allocator state whose lifetime ends when the constructor returns.
`MetalPipelineCache.deinit` can free all owned fields using a valid allocator.
A regression test covers repeated create/use/release cycles.
progress_note: 2026-04-08: `doeNativeMetalPipelineCacheCreate` now uses the process-lifetime `std.heap.c_allocator` instead of a stack-local GPA, and `metal_pipeline_cache.zig` now includes a repeated create/use/release regression test that touches allocator-owned manifest state. `zig build dropin -Doptimize=ReleaseFast` succeeds after the change. `zig build test-full` is still blocked by an unrelated existing failure in `tests/core/lean_proof_test.zig` expecting 11 enum variants and finding 19.

evidence.detail: `MetalPipelineCache` stores `allocator` and uses it during teardown and helper operations.
`doeNativeMetalPipelineCacheCreate` now initializes the cache with `std.heap.c_allocator`, which remains valid across cache teardown.
A regression test creates, uses, releases, recreates, and rereleases the exported cache while registering owned compute keys.

summary: The 999-line Zig source-file policy is mechanically enforced again in the runtime build graph.
why: Once a policy stops being machine-checked, it turns into historical intent instead of an engineering constraint. The checker is back in the build graph, and the temporary WGSL allowlist is now gone because the remaining overages were actually sharded.
exactly_what_to_do: Add a `runtime/zig/tools/check_line_limits.py` script that scans `runtime/zig/src/*.zig` and fails on files over 999 lines.
Wire the new script into `runtime/zig/build.zig` alongside `check_core_import_fence.py` so it runs in normal validation.
Make the script print the file path, current line count, and limit to keep sharding work unambiguous.
acceptance_criteria: `zig build` or the main validation step fails when a source file in `runtime/zig/src` exceeds 999 lines.
The failure message names the offending file and count.
The current three over-limit files are either sharded or temporarily allowlisted with an explicit expiry comment in the checker.
progress_note: 2026-04-08: added `tools/check_line_limits.py`, wired it into the runtime build graph as the `line-limits` step, and made the install/test graph depend on it. After `A003` finished sharding the remaining WGSL overages, the checker now runs with an empty allowlist. `python3 tools/check_line_limits.py`, `zig build line-limits`, and `zig build test-wgsl` all pass on the current tree.

evidence.detail: The style guide states a 999-line maximum for `runtime/zig/src` source files.
The checker scans all Zig source files under `runtime/zig/src`, fails on any overage, and no longer carries a temporary allowlist for the old WGSL test exceptions.
The runtime build graph defines the `line-limits` step and makes the default install graph depend on it.
The main Zig test steps also depend on the line-limit checker before running tests.

summary: The WGSL API, robustness, and emitter test surfaces are sharded back under the 999-line policy, and the line-limit checker no longer needs temporary allowlist entries.
why: The original oversized files made targeted maintenance harder because unrelated robustness cases, API coverage, and emitter checks lived in the same modules. Sharding fixed the policy violation without changing the WGSL suite entrypoint.
exactly_what_to_do: Shard `ir_transform_robustness_test.zig` by transform family or failure mode.
Shard `mod_api_test.zig` by API surface area instead of keeping all translation contract checks in one file.
Shard `shader_emit_test.zig` by backend or emitter family and keep only shared helpers in a common support file.
acceptance_criteria: No file in `runtime/zig/src` exceeds 999 lines.
Each replacement shard has a tight concern boundary that is visible from the filename.
The WGSL test suite still passes from the same entrypoint after sharding.
progress_note: 2026-04-08: `mod_api_test.zig`, `ir_transform_robustness_test.zig`, and `shader_emit_test.zig` were sharded into focused modules below the 999-line cap, then moved into `runtime/zig/tests/wgsl/` with their support files once the split proved stable. `python3 tools/check_line_limits.py`, `zig build line-limits`, and `zig build test-wgsl` all pass, and `tools/check_line_limits.py` runs with an empty allowlist.

evidence.detail: The former monolith is now just the WGSL API test entrypoint that imports focused shards from the dedicated WGSL test subtree.
Translation and backend-baseline cases now live in a dedicated shard below the 999-line cap.
Proof-guided dispatch-precondition cases now live in their own shard below the line limit.
Stage IO and pointer-lowering cases now live in a dedicated shard below the line limit.
Core robustness transform cases remain in the entrypoint file, which now imports a second shard and stays below the cap.
Texture and resource robustness coverage moved into a focused companion shard that also stays below the limit.
The emitter test file now stays under the cap after moving shared builders and cleanup into a support module.
The checker now runs with an empty allowlist because the old WGSL exceptions were removed.

summary: `runtime/zig/src` root is carrying too much mixed responsibility: 150 files and 36,125 LOC sit outside a more explicit feature subtree.
why: This makes the root namespace the de facto dumping ground for runtime APIs, drop-in exports, tools, orchestration, plan executors, and native helpers. That shape fights the style guide's goal of narrow, explicit seams and increases accidental coupling.
exactly_what_to_do: Create explicit subtrees for the biggest root-level clusters, starting with `src/native/`, `src/command/`, `src/plan/`, and `src/cli/`.
Move `doe_*` native ABI files into a native subtree, `command_*` files into a command subtree, and executor/CLI entrypoints into a CLI or plan subtree.
Keep thin compatibility wrappers only where `build.zig` or external symbol names need stable filenames.
acceptance_criteria: New root-level Zig files become rare and justified.
The major root-level families are grouped by subsystem rather than by historical growth.
Import paths become shorter and more intention-revealing inside each subtree.

evidence.detail: Current area totals are 150 files / 36,268 LOC at the source root, versus 92 files / 34,354 LOC in `doe_wgsl` and 112 files / 24,730 LOC in `backend`.
Representative root file that mixes CLI, orchestration, replay, tracing, and artifact work.
Representative root file that mixes backend-specific queue execution and ABI exports.

progress_note: 2026-04-10: created the new src/command/, src/plan/, and src/cli/ subtrees and moved command-kind normalization, plan-executor CLI/emitter helpers, and module-runner CLI/registry logic into them. Root-level native ABI files still need the larger native subtree split.

summary: `doe_queue_submit_native.zig` is a root-level monolith that mixes three backend paths, queue lifecycle, browser-copy helpers, deferred callback plumbing, and local FFI declarations.
why: This file is the clearest example of architectural sag in the native ABI layer. The backend-specific logic is no longer thinly delegated; instead the root file itself owns Metal, Vulkan, and D3D12 details plus unrelated queue lifecycle and browser interop concerns.
exactly_what_to_do: Split backend-specific submit code into `queue_submit_metal.zig`, `queue_submit_vulkan.zig`, and `queue_submit_d3d12.zig`.
Move queue lifecycle and refcount operations into a `queue_lifecycle.zig` shard.
Move browser-copy helpers and external-texture copy paths into dedicated texture-copy shards.
Move D3D12 `extern fn` declarations out of this file into a backend seam module and keep `doe_queue_submit_native.zig` as a thin export/dispatch layer.
acceptance_criteria: The root queue-submit file contains only ABI exports and backend dispatch selection.
Backend-specific GPU work is implemented in backend-owned shards.
Queue lifecycle, work-done callbacks, and browser copy helpers are no longer interleaved in one file.

evidence.detail: The file starts by importing backend bridges and declaring D3D12 `extern fn` symbols locally.
D3D12 and Vulkan submit paths live directly in the root ABI file.
Queue flush, write helpers, lifecycle, deferred callbacks, and browser copy helpers all live in the same file.

progress_note: 2026-04-10: split the root queue submit path into doe_queue_submit_shared.zig, doe_queue_submit_d3d12.zig, doe_queue_submit_metal.zig, doe_queue_lifecycle.zig, and doe_queue_copy_native.zig; doe_queue_submit_native.zig is now a 122-line export/dispatch facade. Vulkan remains an explicit no-op dispatch boundary. zig build test-core, zig build test, and zig build dropin pass.

summary: Backend FFI declarations are spread across too many implementation files: 29 backend files currently own 471 `extern fn` declarations.
why: Typed ABI declarations are a strength in Doe, but their placement is inconsistent. Scattering declarations across command and resource files makes the FFI seam harder to audit, harder to regenerate, and easier to duplicate or drift.
exactly_what_to_do: Create or expand per-backend `bridge_decls.zig` modules that own raw `extern fn` declarations.
Keep implementation files importing typed decl modules instead of declaring bridge calls locally.
Group declarations by domain inside the seam layer when a backend is too large for one file, for example `bridge_render.zig`, `bridge_copy.zig`, and `bridge_surface.zig`.
acceptance_criteria: Raw `extern fn` declarations are concentrated in backend seam modules instead of scattered across feature implementation files.
Implementation files import typed bridge declarations rather than declaring them.
New backend FFI additions have one obvious place to land.

evidence.detail: One implementation file alone owns a large block of raw D3D12 bridge declarations.
Even backend `mod.zig` currently carries raw bridge declarations.
Current measured spread is 29 backend files with 471 total `extern fn` declarations.

progress_note: 2026-04-10: concentrated backend raw extern declarations into seam modules, most notably backend/d3d12/d3d12_bridge_decls.zig and backend/metal/*_bridge_decls.zig. Current backend spread is 5 backend files with 240 extern declarations, and implementation files now import typed bridge modules instead of owning scattered D3D12/Metal declaration blocks. python3 runtime/zig/tools/check_core_import_fence.py, zig build test-core, and zig build test pass.

summary: `wgpu_dropin_lib.zig` mixes config parsing, environment-sensitive behavior selection, global state, error reporting, and a giant compile-time symbol router in one file.
why: This creates a broad, high-churn root file on the drop-in ABI boundary. It also makes configuration behavior harder to test because JSON parsing, lane override lookup, symbol ownership policy, and native proc routing all sit in the same module.
exactly_what_to_do: Move behavior-config parsing and symbol-ownership parsing into a dedicated drop-in config module with cached immutable state.
Replace the giant `resolveDoeNativeProc` ladder with generated tables or at least per-family shards (`instance`, `device`, `buffer`, `queue`, `render`, `surface`).
Keep `wgpu_dropin_lib.zig` focused on exported loading, route selection, and library state ownership.
acceptance_criteria: The main drop-in loader file stops owning the full symbol-routing ladder.
Behavior config and lane override parsing are implemented once and cached explicitly.
The exported loader module is materially smaller and easier to audit.

evidence.detail: Behavior config parsing currently happens inline with an arena on `page_allocator`.
Symbol-ownership config initialization uses global state and direct allocator calls in the loader file.
Lane-based mode resolution reads the environment directly inside the loader file.
`resolveDoeNativeProc` is a very large symbol-to-native-proc ladder.

progress_note: 2026-04-10: moved drop-in behavior parsing and symbol-ownership caching into src/dropin/dropin_runtime_config.zig, moved the native proc resolver to src/dropin/dropin_proc_manifest.zig, and reduced wgpu_dropin_lib.zig to loader/route ownership concerns. zig build dropin passes.

summary: `main.zig` mixes sample data, CLI parsing, file IO, command parsing, replay loading, execution setup, trace buffering, and artifact finalization in one 861-line file.
why: This is one of the main workflow surfaces for contributors, so every added knob or artifact field increases the blast radius of one already-dense file. The seams are present conceptually but not represented in the source tree.
exactly_what_to_do: Extract CLI argument parsing into `main_args.zig` or `runtime_cli_args.zig`.
Extract workload/input loading into a dedicated loader module.
Extract trace-summary assembly and artifact finalization into their own modules.
Leave `main.zig` as a thin orchestration entrypoint that wires together parsed options, loaded inputs, execution, and emitters.
acceptance_criteria: `main.zig` is reduced to orchestration and does not own the full argument ladder or trace-summary layout.
Argument validation, input loading, and artifact writing are individually testable.
Future runtime flags can be added without editing a giant `if/else if` block in `main.zig`.

evidence.detail: The file owns built-in sample quirk and command data.
The CLI argument ladder is implemented inline in `main`.
Input loading, replay parsing, metadata handling, and executor setup all live in the same function.
Artifact recorder setup and trace-summary assembly also live in the same file.

summary: `webgpu_plan_executor.zig` mixes dynamic-library executor construction, command execution, dry-run synthesis, trace/meta writers, argument parsing, and a `main` entrypoint.
why: The file currently acts as library, executor, formatter, serializer, and CLI. That shape hides stable seams that should be explicit: executor core, trace emitters, plan validation, and command-line handling.
exactly_what_to_do: Split executor state and WebGPU object lifecycle into an executor-core module.
Move `writeTraceMeta` and `writeTraceJsonl` into dedicated emitter modules.
Move `parseArgs` and the `main` entrypoint into a thin CLI wrapper module.
Keep `runPlan` as the main library surface and keep the CLI wrapper importing it.
acceptance_criteria: `webgpu_plan_executor.zig` no longer contains its own CLI parser and `main` function.
Trace/meta writing is reusable without importing executor internals.
Executor construction and command execution live in a dedicated core module.

evidence.detail: `Executor` init/deinit owns dynamic library and WebGPU object lifecycle.
The same file also owns command execution for buffer writes, loads, and dispatches.
`runPlan`, summary construction, and trace emitters live here too.
CLI parsing and `main` also live in the same file even though `src/main_webgpu_plan_executor.zig` already exists as a wrapper.

progress_note: 2026-04-10: moved trace/meta emitters into src/plan/webgpu_plan_executor_emit.zig and CLI parsing into src/plan/webgpu_plan_executor_cli.zig, with main_webgpu_plan_executor.zig delegating to the CLI module. Executor core still lives in webgpu_plan_executor.zig, so the row is not fully closed yet. zig build webgpu-plan-executor passes.

summary: `module_runner.zig` dispatches modules with a long repeated `if` ladder that duplicates the same parse/execute/stringify pattern for every module.
why: This is a classic place where a rule-map or registry is a better fit than branching. The repeated pattern increases edit surface every time a module is added and makes it easy to drift in per-module behavior.
exactly_what_to_do: Define a small compile-time registry of module adapters, each exposing `module_id`, `parseRequest`, `parsePolicy`, and `execute`.
Replace the `if` ladder with a lookup over that registry.
Keep per-module parse-policy lifetime management inside adapter-specific helpers so the runner stays thin.
acceptance_criteria: Adding a new module does not require another large copy-paste branch in `main`.
The runner contains one generic execution flow instead of repeated parse/execute/write sequences.
Unsupported-module errors still remain explicit and deterministic.

evidence.detail: The runner starts with a root-level arena on `page_allocator` and owns the entire dispatch path.
Each module branch repeats the same parse-request, parse-policy, execute, stringify, and write pattern.

progress_note: 2026-04-10: replaced the repeated module if-ladder with a registry-backed CLI runner in src/cli/module_runner_cli.zig and turned src/module_runner.zig into a thin wrapper. zig build module-core-runner passes.

summary: `command_json.zig` mixes command-kind normalization, command-family parsing, payload cleanup, and a large inline test block in one file.
why: The parser has grown beyond a single cohesive concern. The command-kind alias table, copy-resource parsing, dispatch parsing, and tests now compete for the same file budget and mental budget.
exactly_what_to_do: Split kind normalization and alias handling into its own module.
Split command-family parsing into dedicated files such as `command_parse_copy.zig`, `command_parse_dispatch.zig`, and `command_parse_render.zig`.
Move the heavy test block into `command_json_test.zig` or `runtime/zig/tests/core/` while keeping only tiny invariants inline if needed.
acceptance_criteria: The production parser file contains only parser code, not a long tail of tests.
Alias handling and command-family parsing are independently testable.
Future command additions touch a narrow shard instead of one large file.

evidence.detail: The production parser logic spans parsing, cleanup, kind normalization, and family-specific parsing.
A substantial inline test block lives in the same source file.

progress_note: 2026-04-10: moved command-kind normalization into src/command/command_kind.zig and moved the heavy inline tests into runtime/zig/tests/core/command_json_test.zig. Command-family parsing still needs narrower shards, so this row remains partial. zig build test-core passes.

summary: Backend contract-path harness logic now lives under `runtime/zig/tests`, and the production backend `mod.zig` files no longer embed test-only profiles or `std.testing.allocator` usage.
why: Small inline tests are idiomatic in Zig, but backend `mod.zig` files had grown test harness helpers that were not part of runtime behavior. Moving that logic back under `tests` restores a cleaner separation between runtime code and contract scaffolding.
exactly_what_to_do: Move contract-path helpers into test support modules under `runtime/zig/tests/backend/` or backend-local `*_test_support.zig` files that are only imported from tests.
Keep production `mod.zig` files focused on interface construction, execution, and telemetry.
Retain tiny inline invariants only when they genuinely exercise a local helper with no external scaffolding.
acceptance_criteria: Backend `mod.zig` files no longer depend on `std.testing.allocator` or test-only profiles.
Test harness logic is imported only from test entrypoints.
Production backend modules read as runtime code, not runtime-plus-test support.
progress_note: 2026-04-08: removed `run_contract_path_for_test` from the Metal, Vulkan, and D3D12 backend modules; added test-only support modules under `runtime/zig/tests/vulkan/` and `runtime/zig/tests/d3d12/`; and updated the integration/timing tests to call those support helpers instead. Repository search no longer finds `std.testing.allocator` inside the production backend `mod.zig` files, and `zig build test-core` advances past the harness split before stopping on the unrelated existing `tests/core/lean_proof_test.zig` enum-count assertion.

evidence.detail: The Vulkan contract-path test helper now lives in a test-only support module and wraps the public backend interface for tests.
The D3D12 contract-path test helper now lives in a test-only support module and preserves the non-Windows unsupported result contract there.
The production Metal backend module now proceeds directly from runtime helpers into the vtable without embedded contract-test helpers or test-only skip logic.
The production Vulkan backend module no longer exposes a contract-test entrypoint; runtime command execution now starts at the backend vtable path.
The production D3D12 backend module no longer embeds the contract-test helper and now proceeds from runtime helpers directly into the backend vtable.

summary: `backend/vulkan/mod.zig` is at 841 lines and mixes backend init, artifact telemetry, capability declaration, command dispatch, buffer capture, and test helper code.
why: The file is still under the hard cap, but it is already acting as both runtime adapter and a catchall for Vulkan backend behavior. That shape will keep trending back over the limit unless it is split before the next feature wave.
exactly_what_to_do: Split Vulkan backend interface assembly from command execution.
Move per-command-family execution into dedicated shards such as `vulkan_execute_copy.zig`, `vulkan_execute_dispatch.zig`, and `vulkan_execute_surface.zig`.
Keep `mod.zig` as the backend facade that wires vtable functions to narrower helpers.
acceptance_criteria: `backend/vulkan/mod.zig` becomes a facade file instead of a large mixed implementation file.
Per-command-family code lives in narrow execution shards.
Artifact telemetry and runtime bootstrapping no longer share the same file as the full command switch.

evidence.detail: Backend struct construction and artifact-manifest wiring live in the same file.
Most Vulkan command execution also lives in the same file.
Test helper, vtable plumbing, prewarm helpers, and buffer capture are also included here.

progress_note: 2026-04-10: moved Vulkan command execution into backend/vulkan/backend_execute.zig and reduced backend/vulkan/mod.zig to a 369-line facade responsible for backend assembly, artifact telemetry, and vtable wiring. zig build test and zig build dropin pass.

summary: `backend/metal/mod.zig` is at 827 lines and mixes backend init, host-plan artifact plumbing, capability declaration, command execution, buffer capture, and test helper code.
why: Like the Vulkan backend facade, the Metal backend module is carrying too many concerns. It is still manageable today, but the file shape already predicts another monolith unless the splits happen intentionally.
exactly_what_to_do: Split backend interface assembly from command-family execution.
Move host-plan artifact plumbing and artifact-manifest helpers into narrower support modules.
Move test helpers out of the backend facade and keep bridge declarations in `metal_bridge_decls.zig` or equivalent seam files.
acceptance_criteria: `backend/metal/mod.zig` reads as a facade file rather than a mixed implementation file.
Command-family logic is delegated to dedicated helpers.
The lone raw bridge declaration in `mod.zig` is moved to a seam module.

evidence.detail: Backend struct init, host-plan artifact plumbing, and artifact-manifest wiring live together.
Most command execution and queue/timestamp configuration logic also live in the same file.
Test helper and buffer-capture logic are included at the end of the same module.

progress_note: 2026-04-10: moved Metal command execution into backend/metal/backend_execute.zig, aligned the bridge declaration surfaces with the current C headers, and reduced backend/metal/mod.zig to a 385-line facade. zig build bridge-manifest, zig build test, and zig build dropin pass.

summary: `doe_vulkan_render_native.zig` mixes texture lifecycle, texture views, samplers, render-pipeline creation, render-pass draw entrypoints, and ad-hoc debug logging in one file.
why: This file is a classic native-ABI accumulation point. As the Vulkan render surface grows, texture, sampler, and render-pass behavior should not need to share a single implementation file or one logging style.
exactly_what_to_do: Split the file into `vulkan_texture_native.zig`, `vulkan_sampler_native.zig`, `vulkan_render_pipeline_native.zig`, and `vulkan_render_pass_native.zig`.
Replace `std.debug.print` warnings with explicit error delivery or structured trace/status propagation appropriate to the native ABI boundary.
Keep only shared type conversions or tiny helpers in a common support shard.
acceptance_criteria: Texture, sampler, and render-pass code no longer live in one file.
The Vulkan native render path stops relying on ad-hoc debug prints for operational outcomes.
The remaining file boundaries mirror cohesive runtime features.

evidence.detail: Texture creation, destruction, and view logic live in one section.
Sampler creation and destruction live in the same file.
Render-pipeline creation and render-pass draw entrypoints also live here.
The file uses `std.debug.print` for runtime failure reporting.

progress_note: 2026-04-10: split doe_vulkan_render_native.zig into doe_vulkan_render_shared.zig, doe_vulkan_texture_native.zig, doe_vulkan_sampler_native.zig, doe_vulkan_render_pipeline_native.zig, and doe_vulkan_render_pass_native.zig; the root file is now a 19-line compatibility facade. The audited runtime warning prints on this path are gone. zig build test and zig build dropin pass.

summary: `full/modules/services/numeric_stability.zig` is a large mixed service module that owns type definitions, validation, math kernels, receipt construction, trace-meta writing, and top-level execution.
why: The file is still under the hard cap, but it already contains several different reasons to change: schema evolution, evaluation logic, file-output behavior, and orchestration. That is a maintenance smell even when behavior is correct.
exactly_what_to_do: Extract the large type/receipt schema block into `numeric_stability_types.zig`.
Extract evaluation helpers into `numeric_stability_eval.zig`.
Extract file and JSON emission into `numeric_stability_io.zig`, and keep `execute` in a thin orchestration module.
acceptance_criteria: Schema/types, evaluation logic, and file emission have separate modules.
`execute` becomes a readable orchestration function rather than the end of a large mixed source file.
Changes to receipt shape do not require editing the same file as the math kernels.

evidence.detail: The file starts with a very large block of request, policy, receipt, and result types.
The same file then implements parsing, validation, math helpers, receipt helpers, and file-writing helpers.
Top-level execution continues in the same file.

summary: The bulky WGSL test corpus has been moved out of `src/doe_wgsl` and into `runtime/zig/tests/wgsl`, leaving no `*_test.zig` files in the implementation subtree.
why: Large dedicated test suites were making the implementation tree harder to navigate and were exacerbating the file-sharding problem inside `src`. Grouping them under `tests/wgsl` restores a clearer separation between implementation and test surfaces while keeping a single WGSL suite entrypoint.
exactly_what_to_do: Move the bulky WGSL golden, coverage, and robustness suites into `runtime/zig/tests/wgsl/` or a dedicated `runtime/zig/src/doe_wgsl/tests/` subtree.
Leave only small API-local tests or invariants close to their implementation files.
Update the WGSL test suite entrypoint to import the moved test modules explicitly.
acceptance_criteria: The production `src/doe_wgsl` tree is dominated by implementation files, not test files.
Large WGSL test suites are grouped together in a test-focused subtree.
WGSL test entrypoints still run from a single suite command after relocation.

evidence.detail: The dedicated WGSL test subtree now contains the full `*_test.zig` corpus for the WGSL frontend and emitters.
The WGSL suite entrypoint imports the dedicated WGSL `*_test.zig` modules from `tests/wgsl` instead of `src/doe_wgsl`.
No `*_test.zig` files remain in `src/doe_wgsl`; the implementation subtree now contains only non-test modules and docs.
`mod.zig` no longer reaches into `runtime/zig/tests/wgsl`; the moved dedicated test subtree is no longer imported from the implementation module.

progress_note: 2026-04-08: moved every remaining `*_test.zig` module out of `runtime/zig/src/doe_wgsl` and into `runtime/zig/tests/wgsl`, updated `test_suite_wgsl.zig` to import the WGSL coverage/emitter/API suites from there, and removed the stale `mod.zig` test-subtree import so normal executable builds stay clean. `zig build test-wgsl` still passes, and `src/doe_wgsl` no longer contains any dedicated test files.

summary: Several audited runtime warning paths now route through explicit error surfaces instead of raw debug prints, but broader non-tooling `std.debug.print` and `std.debug.panic` cleanup still remains across the runtime tree.
why: The style guide and AGENTS rules push runtime-visible behavior toward explicit status/error surfaces and structured trace output. Direct debug printing in runtime paths is unstructured, difficult to gate, and easy to miss in callers.
exactly_what_to_do: Audit runtime-facing `std.debug.print` sites and convert them to explicit error returns, error-scope delivery, or structured trace/status recording.
Leave `std.debug.print` in contributor tools and benchmarks, but isolate those under explicit CLI/tooling subtrees.
Treat `std.debug.panic` in runtime hot paths as a separate pass: keep only truly unrecoverable invariants and route OOM or operational failures through typed errors where possible.
acceptance_criteria: Non-tooling runtime modules no longer use ad-hoc debug printing for ordinary failure handling.
Warning/error outcomes are observable through the runtime's explicit error or trace surfaces.
Bench and CLI tools remain free to print directly without confusing runtime behavior.
progress_note: 2026-04-10: repo search no longer finds std.debug.print under runtime/zig/src/backend, runtime/zig/src/full, or runtime/zig/src/doe_*.zig. Remaining audit debug-call counts are now src_root=75, backend=2, full=0, doe_wgsl=44 and mostly reflect retained panic-only or tool-facing sites rather than ad-hoc warning prints. zig build test-core and zig build test pass.

evidence.detail: The queue-submit path now has a small helper that routes internal failures through the device error-scope stack instead of printing directly.
The audited Vulkan queue flush warning now delivers an internal device error instead of using `std.debug.print`.
The Vulkan render helper now formats internal runtime failures into the device error-scope stack.
Texture and sampler runtime failures on the audited path now report through explicit device error scopes instead of direct prints.
The audited render-pass draw failure sites now route through the device error-scope surface rather than raw debug printing.
Timestamp diagnostics now use a scoped logger instead of ad-hoc `std.debug.print`.

summary: The Metal runtime resource path now takes an explicit optional `*MetalPipelineCache` handle instead of discovering cache participation by probing struct shape.
why: Reflection is sometimes justified, but here it was acting as a substitute for an explicit interface. Making the cache handle part of the helper API makes the relationship easier to follow and harder to break.
exactly_what_to_do: Define a narrow explicit pipeline-cache hook interface for runtime resource users.
Pass an optional typed cache handle or hook table into the resource helpers instead of probing field existence with `@hasField`.
Keep `@hasField`-style reflection restricted to clearly-labeled generic helpers or ABI/meta layers.
acceptance_criteria: The runtime resource path no longer probes struct shape with `@hasField` to discover cache behavior.
Optional pipeline-cache behavior is represented by an explicit type or hook table.
Call sites make cache participation visible in the function signature.
progress_note: 2026-04-08: `metal_runtime_resources.zig` now accepts an explicit optional `*MetalPipelineCache` on compute/render pipeline helpers and PSO resolution, while `metal_native_runtime.zig` threads that handle through warmup and steady-state calls via a narrow `pipelineBinaryCache()` helper. `zig build doe-runtime` and `zig build doe-plan-executor` both pass, and a repository search finds no remaining `@hasField` usage in the Metal runtime/resource path.

evidence.detail: Kernel pipeline creation now takes an explicit optional `*MetalPipelineCache`, uses it for compile-or-serve lookup, and registers cache keys through that typed handle.
Render pipeline resolution now also takes an explicit optional cache handle instead of reading optional cache participation from the runtime struct shape.
The internal compute/render PSO helpers now accept `device` plus optional typed cache handle, with no reflective `@hasField` checks remaining.
Warmup and steady-state runtime call sites now make cache participation visible by passing the typed handle returned from `pipelineBinaryCache()`.

summary: The two plan-executor library modules now expose `runCli()` and leave `pub fn main()` to thin wrappers, but contributor-facing entrypoints are still scattered through `runtime/zig/src`.
why: This is not a correctness bug, but it contributes to root-level clutter and makes the runtime source tree look more mixed than it needs to. The build already uses thin wrappers for some executables, so the code shape can follow that boundary more consistently.
exactly_what_to_do: Create a dedicated `src/cli/` or `src/tools/` subtree for contributor-facing executables.
Move wrapper-only entrypoints there and rename internal library `main` functions to `runCli` or similar where wrappers already exist.
Keep runtime/library modules importable without also being executable entrypoints.
acceptance_criteria: Contributor tools are grouped in a dedicated subtree instead of scattered across `runtime/zig/src` root.
Wrapper entrypoints are the only files that expose `pub fn main()` where a wrapper already exists.
Library modules can be read as libraries without carrying command-line concerns.
progress_note: 2026-04-10: contributor-facing module-runner logic now lives under src/cli/module_runner_cli.zig, and direct WebGPU plan-executor CLI parsing now lives under src/plan/webgpu_plan_executor_cli.zig. Wrapper entrypoints are still present at the root for build-stable filenames, so the grouping cleanup is still incomplete.

evidence.detail: The measured `pub fn main()` count inside `runtime/zig/src` is now 10 after removing the duplicate plan-executor library entrypoints.
The Doe plan executor wrapper remains the only `main()` for that executable and delegates to `runCli()`.
The direct WebGPU plan executor wrapper remains the only `main()` for that executable and delegates to `runCli()`.
The library module now exposes `runCli()` instead of its own `main()` entrypoint.
The library module now exposes `runCli()` instead of its own `main()` entrypoint.

summary: First-party Doe host code still has no portable-SIMD `@Vector` adoption, so CPU-side preprocessing, tracing, and numeric helpers are leaving cross-target throughput on the table.
why: Doe does significant backend-agnostic CPU work before driver execution. Zig can express one vectorized source path and let LLVM lower it to AVX2, AVX-512, or NEON, but the current tree is still scalar in the hot candidates that run on every platform.
exactly_what_to_do: Add a small `runtime/zig/src/runtime/simd/` layer with scalar-plus-`@Vector` helpers for byte classification, escape scanning, numeric reduce/dot, and aligned chunk compare/copy.
Keep scalar reference implementations as the contract path and route to SIMD only when alignment and length gates are satisfied.
Add target-keyed benchmark artifacts so scalar versus SIMD paths can be compared on x86_64 and arm64 before wider rollout.
acceptance_criteria: First-party `runtime/zig/src` includes at least one non-test `@Vector` fast path with a scalar fallback.
Host-path benchmark artifacts report scalar versus SIMD results with CPU and target metadata.
SIMD adoption does not change trace, parser, or numeric result semantics.

evidence.detail: Repository search found no first-party `@Vector` usage under `runtime/zig/src`; current SIMD-related hits are GPU subgroup/MSL emission rather than CPU host fast paths.
Trace JSON escaping and status-code normalization are scalar byte loops.
Lexer whitespace/comment skipping and numeric scanning are scalar byte walks.
Pairwise numeric helpers are scalar loops with repeated temporary allocation.

summary: `doe_wgsl` lexing remains byte-at-a-time for whitespace, comment skipping, identifier runs, and numeric scans even though those loops are prime portable-SIMD candidates.
why: Shader ingest is backend-agnostic front-end work that runs before any native driver path. Fast chunk scanning can reduce compile overhead without committing Doe to x86-only intrinsics or backend-specific code paths.
exactly_what_to_do: Introduce vectorized helpers for whitespace classification, slash-star/slash-slash probe windows, digit runs, and identifier continuation tests.
Keep scalar tokenization logic as the reference path and fall back automatically on short tails or tricky boundary cases.
Benchmark lex throughput on representative WGSL corpora before and after the SIMD fast path.
acceptance_criteria: `Lexer.skipWhitespaceAndComments`, `lexNumber`, or `lexIdent` uses a shared SIMD-aware scan helper with scalar fallback.
Lexer tests still pass unchanged from the same suite entrypoint.
Benchmark artifacts show whether SIMD helps on large and small shader bodies.

evidence.detail: Whitespace and nested comment skipping currently advances one byte at a time.
Numeric literal scanning is implemented as scalar digit and suffix walks.
Identifier scanning is a scalar continuation loop.

summary: Trace-string escaping and status-code normalization still operate one byte at a time across every artifact row, which is leaving easy host-side wins in a path Doe exercises heavily for gates and benchmarking.
why: Artifact generation is part of Doe's process contract, so its overhead matters. Zig can scan for escape-worthy or non-alphanumeric bytes in vector-sized chunks while preserving the exact JSONL and status-code outputs.
exactly_what_to_do: Add a shared SIMD-aware byte-scan helper that quickly finds the next escape or separator boundary in a slice.
Use that helper in `trace.writeJsonString` and `normalizeExecutionStatusCode`, keeping the existing scalar formatting behavior for the slow path.
Deduplicate any parallel string-escaping helpers in tooling modules so the same fast path is reused consistently.
acceptance_criteria: Trace string escaping and status normalization share a reusable SIMD-aware scan helper with scalar fallback.
Generated trace/meta artifacts remain byte-for-byte compatible with current schemas for unchanged inputs.
A microbenchmark covers short, medium, and long strings with mixed escaping density.

evidence.detail: JSON string escaping is a scalar byte loop that branches on every byte.
Execution-status normalization is also a scalar classification loop over the full string.
Trace row emitters repeatedly call the same string-escaping path while building artifact rows.

summary: The numeric-stability runtime still performs scalar pairwise dot and reduction helpers with fresh temporary allocations, even though this is one of the clearest CPU-compute candidates for Zig vectorization and scratch-buffer reuse.
why: This path is ordinary host-side math, not GPU execution. Zig can pair explicit scratch ownership with portable SIMD to improve throughput while keeping a deterministic scalar reference implementation for verification and debugging.
exactly_what_to_do: Add an explicit scratch-buffer owner for `pairwiseDotF32` and `pairwiseReduceF32` so repeated calls stop allocating fresh arrays in the hot path.
Implement a vectorized block dot/reduction path over f32 chunks, then fall back to the existing scalar tail handling.
Keep the serial f64 path as the reference mode and benchmark precision and speed tradeoffs explicitly.
acceptance_criteria: `pairwiseDotF32` and `pairwiseReduceF32` no longer allocate fresh temporary slices on every call in the steady state.
A SIMD-backed f32 path exists alongside the scalar reference implementation.
Bench artifacts report throughput and numeric-difference envelopes for the same workloads.

evidence.detail: Pairwise dot and reduction allocate temporaries and sum scalars today.
Attention/logit evaluation repeatedly calls the pairwise helpers inside larger loops.

summary: Tooling replay loading now uses one explicit owner for the artifact buffer, parsed expectations, and parse arena instead of duplicating per-row strings.
why: CLI, plan-executor, trace, and replay code are where Zig's arena model pays off most cleanly. These paths benefit from deterministic bulk teardown without touching GPU hot-path semantics.
exactly_what_to_do: Formalize an arena-per-run policy for tooling entrypoints and document when slices may safely alias an owned input blob.
Convert replay and similar artifact ingesters to prefer offsets or borrowed slices into one owned buffer when the data lifetime matches the artifact lifetime.
Keep long-lived runtime objects on explicit allocators and reserve arenas for request-scoped or run-scoped data.
acceptance_criteria: Top-level tooling paths create one explicit run arena or equivalent scoped owner for transient parse state.
Replay and similar loaders avoid per-field duplication when the artifact buffer lifetime already dominates the parsed records.
Repeated tool invocations can init and deinit cleanly under tests without leak noise.
progress_note: 2026-04-08: added `ReplayExpectationSet` as the replay loader owner, switched `parseReplayLine` to borrow validated slices, updated `main.zig` to hold and deinit the owner at run scope, and documented the run-scoped tooling allocation rule in `runtime/zig/README.md`. `zig test src/replay.zig` passes, and `zig build test-core` now advances past the replay tests before stopping on the unrelated existing `tests/core/lean_proof_test.zig` enum-count assertion.

evidence.detail: `module_runner` already uses an arena for process-scoped argument and request handling.
`loadKernelRoot` already uses a local arena for JSON parsing before duplicating the selected result.
Replay loading now owns one artifact buffer plus a local parse arena and returns borrowed `command`/`kernel` slices inside a single `ReplayExpectationSet`.
A focused test verifies that replay expectations keep simple field slices inside the owned artifact buffer instead of allocating duplicate per-row strings.
The CLI now keeps replay expectations under one run-scoped owner and deinitializes them explicitly after the run.
The runtime README now records the run-scoped tooling allocation rule and when parsed records may alias an owned input blob.

summary: Allocator injection is common in Doe, but several modules still hide GPA or `c_allocator` roots in file globals or exported constructors instead of making long-lived ownership explicit at subsystem boundaries.
why: This weakens lifetime reasoning and creates room for repeats of the pipeline-cache allocator bug. Zig's allocator model is strongest when the owner of a long-lived allocation root is visible in the API and teardown path.
exactly_what_to_do: Inventory process-lifetime and subsystem-lifetime allocator roots, including file-global GPAs, `c_allocator` queues, and constructor-local GPAs.
Move long-lived allocators behind explicit init/deinit owners or clearly documented singleton modules with repeatable teardown semantics.
Add focused tests that create, use, destroy, and recreate the affected subsystems to catch allocator-lifetime drift.
acceptance_criteria: No exported constructor or subsystem silently creates long-lived GPA or `c_allocator` state without an explicit owner.
Process-lifetime singletons are documented as such and are mechanically safe to reinitialize where tests require it.
Allocator-root policy is consistent enough that A001-style lifetime bugs become harder to introduce.

evidence.detail: `multi_adapter` keeps a file-global GPA allocator root.
`multi_queue` uses a file-global GPA for exported native handles.
The task pool allocates threads and job nodes directly from `c_allocator` at singleton scope.
The current pipeline-cache constructor still demonstrates the hidden-root lifetime hazard directly.

summary: The task pool and pipeline single-flight registry both use linked-list heap nodes, which is a poor fit for the cache-friendly, explicit-layout style Zig is good at when these coordination paths are exercised under load.
why: Pointer-chasing queues add allocator traffic, branchy traversal, and poor locality. Doe's host orchestration paths can use flatter ring-buffer, slab, or indexed-wait-list designs without changing external behavior.
exactly_what_to_do: Replace per-job linked-list allocation in the task pool with a bounded ring or reusable freelist so steady-state submission stops allocating per job.
Replace single-flight waiter chains with a flatter structure such as hash-map buckets plus slab indices or reusable node pools.
Benchmark queue throughput, join/take latency, and contention behavior before and after the layout change.
acceptance_criteria: The task-pool submit path no longer allocates one fresh heap node per job in steady state.
Single-flight join/take no longer depends on pointer-chasing linked lists for every waiter path.
Bench artifacts show the effect of the flatter layout on coordination overhead.

evidence.detail: The global task-pool state is built around linked-list head and tail pointers.
Worker completion destroys one heap node per job.
Each submit allocates a fresh `JobNode` before enqueue.
Single-flight entries and waiters are stored as linked nodes.
Join and take traverse pointer chains rather than flatter indexed storage.

summary: Doe already uses compile-time reflection well for command partitions and coverage ledgers, but the drop-in proc resolver is still a hand-maintained `if (comptime std.mem.eql(...))` ladder that should be generated from one manifest.
why: Manual symbol routing scales poorly and makes ABI coverage drift easy to miss. Zig can keep the mapping explicit while generating the resolver and compile-time exhaustiveness checks from one source of truth.
exactly_what_to_do: Define one manifest that records WebGPU symbol name, owning Doe implementation, and any non-native bridge owner.
Generate `resolveDoeNativeProc` and related diagnostics tables with `inline for` over that manifest instead of growing the string ladder manually.
Add compile-time checks that fail when a declared exported symbol is missing from the manifest or mapped to the wrong function type.
acceptance_criteria: `resolveDoeNativeProc` no longer relies on a large hand-written equality ladder for most symbols.
The proc-routing manifest is checked at compile time for coverage and function-type consistency.
Diagnostics and ownership reporting reuse the same manifest instead of a second manual table.

evidence.detail: The core surface already uses compile-time reflection and `inline for` to derive command metadata.
The full surface does the same for full-only command ledgers.
Command partition invariants are already enforced at compile time.
The drop-in proc resolver is still a long hand-maintained compile-time string ladder.

progress_note: 2026-04-10: replace the handwritten resolveDoeNativeProc ladder with the tuple-manifest in src/dropin/dropin_proc_manifest.zig plus compile-time duplicate-symbol checks. Ownership diagnostics still do not reuse the same manifest, so the row remains partial. zig build dropin passes.

summary: Backend bridge declarations are still mostly hand-written extern inventories even though Doe already carries large stable bridge surfaces that could be generated or machine-checked from a manifest.
why: Hundreds of manual extern declarations across backends are explicit but review-heavy and drift-prone. Zig can preserve explicit ABI boundaries while generating declarations, symbol inventories, or header-parity checks from one source of truth.
exactly_what_to_do: Start with one backend, preferably Metal, and define a machine-readable bridge manifest that records symbol name, signature owner, and source file.
Generate the Zig extern declarations or at minimum a parity-check artifact from that manifest, then wire the check into the normal build/validation path.
Reuse the same pattern for D3D12 and Vulkan bridge surfaces once the first backend proves out.
acceptance_criteria: At least one backend bridge surface is generated from or mechanically checked against a manifest.
Build or validation fails when bridge declaration drift is introduced.
The manifest is explicit enough to review ABI changes without scanning large declaration files by hand.

evidence.detail: The audit meta row records 471 extern function declarations across 29 backend files.
Metal alone carries a large hand-written extern inventory.
The runtime already builds shared-library and CLI artifacts through Zig's native toolchain, so bridge checks can live in the same build graph.

progress_note: 2026-04-10: added runtime/zig/tools/bridge_manifests/metal_bridge_manifest.json, wired the bridge-manifest build step, and extended the checked Metal declaration surface to cover device-enumeration and binary-archive symbols. zig build bridge-manifest passes.

summary: Doe concurrency is thread-pool and worker-callback based today; the repo has not yet adopted an explicit uncolored I/O context for tooling and orchestration paths, which is where Zig's sync versus cooperative versus threaded split would fit best.
why: Tooling, trace IO, and plan executors are good fits for explicit sync, cooperative same-thread, and real parallel modes, while GPU hot paths should stay on the existing runtime threading contract. Making that split explicit would reduce ad hoc coordination code without hiding behavior behind runtime magic.
exactly_what_to_do: Prototype one tooling path with an injected I/O context so the same logic can run in plain synchronous mode first and alternate modes later without signature forks.
Map cooperative same-thread work to the async/cooperative lane and OS-thread parallel work to the concurrent lane, while keeping queue and device execution on the current explicit threading contract.
Document which tooling paths may block, cooperate on one thread, or fan out onto worker threads so concurrency semantics stay explicit.
acceptance_criteria: One tooling subsystem can run under a plain synchronous context and at least one alternate I/O context without API duplication.
Docs describe cooperative same-thread versus OS-thread parallel semantics and keep GPU execution out of the new abstraction.
The threading contract for runtime objects remains explicit and unchanged unless a follow-up row records that change.

evidence.detail: The current strategy is framed around background jobs, async pipeline creation, and worker-pool tuning rather than an I/O-context abstraction.
Current runtime semantics are expressed as explicit object thread classes and worker-thread callbacks.
The concrete implementation today is a thread-based worker pool with condition-variable wakeups, not an uncolored I/O surface.
Repository search found no current `std.Io`, `io.async()`, or `io.concurrent()` usage in first-party Doe code.

summary: A dedicated executor-side sticky-state cache is no longer the active fix path for package encode cost on the governed Gemma package slice.
why: Gemma-270M package plans are dominated by repeated compute dispatches with one bind group per dispatch, but the direct package bottleneck turned out to be host/native call count rather than executor-side state comparisons alone. The original sticky-state experiment regressed badly, and the later fused dispatch path removed most of the encode cost without relying on this row's original approach.
exactly_what_to_do: Leave the executor-side sticky-state idea parked unless a future strict-comparable artifact shows repeated-state rebinding dominating again after the fused helper path.
If this row is resumed, compare it only against the fused-dispatch baseline rather than the older three-call path.
Keep strict comparability and per-sample trace-meta breakdowns enabled on any follow-up experiment.
acceptance_criteria: Consecutive dispatches with identical pipeline and bind-group state do not emit redundant package API calls if this row is resumed.
Node and Bun package compares remain `comparisonStatus=comparable` on the Gemma-270M Vulkan lane.
Package `executionEncodeTotalNs` decreases measurably relative to the fused-dispatch baseline, not the retired three-call path.
progress_note: 2026-04-08: the first executor-side sticky-state experiment regressed both strict package compares instead of helping. The later fused `_dispatchBound` path at `20260408T184531Z` and `20260408T184545Z` cut Doe package encode cost to the low-millisecond class without reviving sticky-state caching, so this row is now parked rather than active.

evidence.detail: The executor now routes the hot dispatch path through `_dispatchBound(...)` when available instead of issuing three separate package API calls unconditionally.
The executor-side cache experiment regressed Node package p50 to about 301.7ms versus Dawn about 159.3ms while remaining strict-comparable.
The same executor-side cache experiment regressed Bun package p50 to about 244.6ms versus Dawn about 176.7ms while remaining strict-comparable.
With the fused dispatch path in place, Node strict package compare records Doe p50 `executionEncodeTotalNs` in the same low-millisecond class as Dawn while remaining comparable.
The same fused path materially reduces Bun package encode time while strict comparability remains green.

summary: A fused internal compute-dispatch helper removed the dominant Node/Bun package encode overhead on the governed Gemma-270M Vulkan slice.
why: After the shared single-submit fast path removed the submit bottleneck, direct compute encoding was still paying several addon/FFI crossings per dispatch. Routing the common single-bind-group shape through one `_dispatchBound(...)` helper collapsed the Node encode gap and materially reduced the Bun encode gap while preserving strict comparability.
exactly_what_to_do: Keep the strict-comparable direct encoder path, but add a fused or lower-call-count compute encode primitive for the common single-bind-group dispatch shape.
Use the existing plan-shape constraints to route only the simple hot path to the fused encoder helper and keep the generic path for immediates, indirect dispatch, and uncommon descriptors.
Measure Node and Bun package encode cost before and after the fused path on the Gemma-270M Vulkan slice.
acceptance_criteria: The common compute-dispatch package path emits fewer host/native boundary crossings per dispatch.
Node and Bun package `executionEncodeTotalNs` both improve on the Gemma-270M Vulkan workload.
Strict comparability remains green and the direct-encoder replay mismatch does not return.
progress_note: 2026-04-08: landed `_dispatchBound(...)` in the shared encoder surface, added direct Node and Bun backend support, added a native `doeNativeComputePassDispatchBound` bridge path, and switched the governed executor hot path to use it. The strict reruns at `20260408T184531Z` and `20260408T184545Z` cut Doe package encode from the prior tens-of-milliseconds class to the low-millisecond class while remaining `comparisonStatus=comparable`. Node is now in the same encode class as Dawn on this slice, and Bun is materially closer.

evidence.detail: The shared encoder surface now exposes `_dispatchBound(...)` for the common single-bind-group compute hot path.
Node direct compute encode now uses one fused addon call with a fallback instead of always issuing separate pipeline, bind-group, and dispatch calls.
Bun direct compute encode now uses one fused FFI call with a fallback instead of always issuing separate pipeline, bind-group, and dispatch calls.
The addon exposes `computePassDispatchBound`, which uses the native fused path when available and falls back to the three-call sequence otherwise.
The governed package executor now routes the dispatch hot path through `_dispatchBound(...)` when the backend exposes it.
Node strict package compare records Doe p50 `executionEncodeTotalNs` around 3.0ms versus Dawn about 3.1ms while Doe overall remains faster on the slice.
Bun strict package compare records Doe p50 `executionEncodeTotalNs` around 4.1ms versus Dawn about 1.9ms while Doe overall remains faster on the slice.

summary: The earlier package-lane submit regression is no longer explained by runtime Vulkan replay; after the shared submit fast path landed, replay-path work is no longer the active bottleneck on the Gemma-270M package slice.
why: Three runtime replay experiments all regressed badly, and the later shared package submit fix collapsed Doe p50 `executionSubmitWaitTotalNs` from about 121.6ms to about 3.3ms on Node and from about 125.1ms to about 4.0ms on Bun. That means the runtime Vulkan replay seam is not the currently limiting path on this workload.
exactly_what_to_do: Keep the current Vulkan package submit behavior and do not resume replay-path experimentation unless a new strict-comparable artifact shows runtime submit cost returning as the dominant gap.
Use the `20260408T182027Z` package artifacts as the new submit baseline when evaluating future runtime submit ideas.
Focus the next package optimization round on `A032` encode overhead, not on queue-submit replay.
acceptance_criteria: Strict Node and Bun package compares continue to show single-digit-millisecond `executionSubmitWaitTotalNs` for Doe on this slice.
No new runtime replay experiment is accepted unless a strict-comparable artifact first shows submit-side runtime cost dominating again.
The active package-performance backlog moves to encode/bridge work instead of replay restore work.
progress_note: 2026-04-08: two naive Vulkan batch attempts (`20260408T174357Z`, `20260408T174542Z`) and one replay-restore attempt (`20260408T180031Z`, `20260408T180121Z`) were all reverted after large regressions. The later package-level direct submit fix at `20260408T182027Z` made submit/wait effectively disappear on the governed Gemma package lane, so this row is now a parked hypothesis rather than the next implementation target.

evidence.detail: The current Vulkan queue-submit shim still returns immediately, and the reverted replay-restore experiment on this seam did not produce a win.
The first naive Vulkan batch experiment regressed strict Node package p50 to about 931.6ms versus Dawn about 164.7ms.
A follow-up retained-state tweak still regressed strict Node package p50 to about 917.9ms versus Dawn about 165.8ms.
The replay-restore attempt kept Node strict-comparable but regressed Doe p50 to about 251.6ms versus Dawn about 158.5ms.
The same replay-restore attempt kept Bun strict-comparable but regressed Doe p50 to about 254.5ms versus Dawn about 162.9ms.
After the shared package submit fix, Node strict compare records Doe p50 `executionSubmitWaitTotalNs` around 3.3ms and `submitQueueSubmitTotalNs` around 1.1ms.
After the same fix, Bun strict compare records Doe p50 `executionSubmitWaitTotalNs` around 4.0ms and `submitQueueSubmitTotalNs` around 1.05ms.

summary: Shared single-command-buffer submit packing in the package drop-in layer was the dominant Node/Bun `queue.submit` bottleneck, and direct timing plus fast paths removed it on the governed Gemma-270M Vulkan slice.
why: The strict reruns first at `20260408T182027Z` and then again after the encode fix at `20260408T184531Z` and `20260408T184545Z` keep Doe package submit in the low-millisecond class on both Node and Bun. The common package bottleneck was in submit packing and bridge overhead, not in hidden runtime queue work.
exactly_what_to_do: Keep the direct-submit breakdown fields on the Node and Bun package lanes so future regressions show whether time returns in packing, submit, or bookkeeping.
Preserve the single-command-buffer fast path in the addon and the reusable pointer scratch path in Bun when future package changes touch `queue.submit`.
Treat `A032` encode overhead as the next package-performance target now that shared submit overhead is reduced.
acceptance_criteria: Strict Node and Bun package compares retain the new direct submit breakdown fields on this slice.
Doe package `submitQueueSubmitTotalNs` remains near the current low-millisecond class on the Gemma-270M Vulkan lane.
Strict comparability stays green while later package work proceeds on top of the fast path.
progress_note: 2026-04-08: direct submit timing in `doe_queue_submit`, Node breakdown capture, and Bun pointer-scratch reuse removed the shared submit bottleneck. The later strict reruns after the encode fix still preserve the low submit class, so this row is closed and future work should treat submit regressions as a new incident rather than reopening this implementation item.

evidence.detail: The addon now times direct package submit and uses a single-command-buffer fast path instead of always heap-packing an array.
Node package submit now records the addon-returned breakdown on the direct path.
Bun package submit now keeps per-run submit breakdown state and reuses pointer scratch storage for direct queue submit.
After the fix, Node strict package compare records Doe p50 `submitQueueSubmitTotalNs` around 1.1ms and overall p50 about 103.2ms versus Dawn about 163.7ms.
After the fix, Bun strict package compare records Doe p50 `submitQueueSubmitTotalNs` around 1.05ms and overall p50 about 102.2ms versus Dawn about 169.6ms.
The later Node strict rerun keeps Doe overall faster while the fused encode fix does not reintroduce a submit bottleneck.
The later Bun strict rerun keeps Doe overall faster while the fused encode fix does not reintroduce a submit bottleneck.
